interest: Equity advocates, teachers' unions, and the reform movement share an interest in ensuring S4 does not become a permanent settlement that legitimizes early sorting while offering only reversible compensation.
value: Equity and structural justice versus political feasibility and near-term general-school quality; also durability of commitment versus fiscal discretion.
fear: That compensation will be presented as a solution, foreclose future structural reform through 'we already fixed it' lock-in, and then be cut in the next fiscal consolidation—leaving disadvantaged pupils sorted out at age 10 with promises that expire.
affected: low-SES and Roma pupils sorted out at age 10
middle-class parents who benefit from selective tracks
future governments bound (or not) by a sunset commitment
the education budget under fiscal consolidation
assumption: That a binding sunset clause plus constitutional funding protection is enforceable across electoral cycles in the Hungarian legal/political environment, and that without it compensation degenerates into permanent legitimization of sorting.
empirical_uncertainty: Contested-to-unknown: no country precedent shows compensation can offset (not merely mask) the effects of sorting as early as age 10 (Portugal did not move its selection age), and the durability of targeted funding under Hungarian fiscal consolidation is empirically doubtful given repeated past cuts.
